metadata.title: Build a JTBD Audience Segment Generator with Claude
metadata.note: cli-ideas Card 18 — Ch 4 (The Brand Audience). DOUBLE-CHECK: Christensen milkshake study — commuters in the morning hired the milkshake to tolerate the boring commute and stay full until lunch without making a mess; real competitor = banana, granola bar, bagel; design implication = thicker, easier dispense, wider straw. A 22-year-old graduate student and a 55-year-old executive can be the same segment if they have the same job. [DEMOGRAPHIC — CONVERT TO JOB] fires on any segment that leads with age/income/occupation. CONFIRMED / NEEDS-EVIDENCE / INFERRED tags required per claim.

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — Claude UI, ask shown answered; IN-FOR-BEAR
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Build it with Claude, then take it apart.

props.command: claude "generate 3 JTBD audience segments — segment by job, not demographics"
output: [DEMOGRAPHIC — CONVERT TO JOB]: 'professional women 28-40 in tech' → converting to job-level segment
Segment 1: 'The accountability builder' — job: audit decisions before a high-stakes meeting
Segment 2: 'The signal restorer' — job: rebuild credibility after a reorg
Cultural tension: clarity valued over confidence [CONFIRMED]

beats.role_note: stakes — BEFORE CLI loop; SHOW-DON'T-TELL: animate the milkshake reframe
beats.narration_text: Clayton Christensen's team was hired to increase milkshake sales at a fast-food chain. The firm segmented by demographics and psychographics in the usual way and found nothing actionable. Christensen reframed: what job are customers hiring the milkshake to do? They observed the purchases directly. The majority were bought by commuters in the morning. The job was not dessert. It was: make the boring commute tolerable and stay full until lunch without making a mess. The real competitor was not other milkshakes. It was a banana, a granola bar, a bagel — anything that could be consumed one-handed on a highway. The design implications changed completely. A 22-year-old graduate student and a 55-year-old executive can be the same segment if they are hired for the same job. Demographics would have separated them. The job unites them.
beats.visual_intent: Animated reframe: demographic segmentation (age bands, occupation boxes) shown as non-actionable → pivot to 'what job does the commuter hire the milkshake to do?' → job: tolerate commute + stay full → real competitor = banana/granola bar; design changes: thicker, easier dispense, wider straw; no composer yet

beats.role_note: ClaudeComposerAsk — show and discuss the prompt
beats.narration_text: Into Claude. Generate three candidate segments defined by job-to-be-done, not demographics. For each: segment name as a phrase naming the job, not age, income, or occupation — if any segment leads with a demographic descriptor, fire the flag demographic, convert to job. Then the specific job: what does someone in this segment hire this brand to do, with a specific occasion and outcome. The cultural tension: what contradiction does this group feel that no brand in the space is currently resolving. One verifiable behavior — specific and observable, not an inferred motive. And evidence status: confirmed, needs-evidence, or inferred.

props.command:
claude "generate 3 JTBD segments for:
  [ARCHETYPE + DATA]
For each segment:
  1. Name: a PHRASE naming the JOB — not age/income.
     If demographic: [DEMOGRAPHIC — CONVERT TO JOB]
  2. Job: what does this person hire the brand to do?
     Specific occasion + outcome.
  3. Cultural tension: what contradiction do they feel
     that no brand here is resolving?
  4. Verifiable behavior: one observable specific action.
  5. Evidence status: CONFIRMED / NEEDS-EVIDENCE / INFERRED"

beats.role_note: ClaudeCodeBeat — actual segment generator; trimmed to what teaches
beats.narration_text: Read it. The demographic detection logic is where the discipline lives: any segment leading with age bracket, income level, or job title fires the flag. The model is pattern-matching on what audiences look like — and that pattern is almost always demographic. The flag forces the reframe. The verifiable behavior field is the other critical output — a claim like 'they check competitor pricing daily' is testable. A claim like 'they care deeply about quality' is not. Evidence tags separate hypothesis from data.

props.title: jtbd_segments.py
props.code:
# jtbd_segments.py  —  Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

SYSTEM = """You are an audience analyst using Jobs-to-Be-Done.
Generate 3 segments. For each:
  1. Name: JOB phrase — not demographic.
     If leading with age/income/title:
     flag [DEMOGRAPHIC — CONVERT TO JOB]
  2. Job: specific occasion + outcome the brand is hired for
  3. Cultural tension: contradiction this group feels
     that no brand in this space is resolving
  4. Verifiable behavior: ONE observable specific thing
     members do — not an inferred motive
  5. Evidence status per claim:
     CONFIRMED | NEEDS-EVIDENCE | INFERRED
Do NOT invent market-size numbers or statistics.
Do NOT accept a segment defined primarily by demographic."""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
result = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7", max_tokens=2048,
    system=SYSTEM,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sys.stdin.read()}]
)
print(result.content[0].text)

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — DEMOGRAPHIC flag fires, then job-level segments
beats.narration_text: Supply a demographic prompt: professional women twenty-eight to forty in tech. The flag fires immediately: demographic, convert to job. The model reframes and produces three job-level segments. Segment one: The accountability builder — hired to audit decisions before a high-stakes meeting. Segment two: The signal restorer — hired to rebuild credibility after a reorg. Each segment has a cultural tension and a verifiable behavior. The evidence tags show two confirmed, one needs-evidence. No market size numbers anywhere in the output.
beats.visual_intent: screen-rec mp4: demographic input typed in → '[DEMOGRAPHIC — CONVERT TO JOB]' flag fires in amber → three segments print with job phrases as headers; cultural tension visible as one-liner; evidence tags (CONFIRMED/NEEDS-EVIDENCE/INFERRED) in color on each claim; no invented statistics.

beats.narration_text: Now pressure-test the verifiable behavior claims. Ask the tool to upgrade the needs-evidence items: for each unverified behavior claim, name the specific evidence type that would confirm it — a forum thread, a support ticket pattern, an interview cohort, a behavioral analytics query. The segment is a hypothesis until the verifiable behavior is actually verified.

props.command:
claude "for the three segments generated:
  For each NEEDS-EVIDENCE item:
    Name the specific evidence type required
    to upgrade it to CONFIRMED:
    - interview quote (how many, what forum?)
    - support ticket pattern (what keyword?)
    - behavioral cohort (what action in analytics?)
    - forum thread (which community?)
  Produce a one-week evidence acquisition plan per segment."

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — evidence acquisition plans per segment
beats.narration_text: Each needs-evidence item now has a specific acquisition path: segment one needs five interviews with engineering managers in communities X and Y; segment two needs a support ticket search for the keyword visibility; segment three needs a forum scrape from a specific subreddit. The segment is no longer just plausible. It has a testable behavioral claim and a plan to confirm it. That is the difference between an audience and a persona invented by a model.
beats.visual_intent: Three-panel: each segment shows 'NEEDS-EVIDENCE → evidence type → one-week acquisition plan'; visual status tag moving from amber (NEEDS-EVIDENCE) to green (path to CONFIRMED); Christensen milkshake comparison at bottom: 'they observed directly, we build the observation plan'

beats.narration_text: The milkshake commuter is the canonical case because the observation was direct and the design implication was specific. Same product, completely different competitive set, completely different design implication — because the job was the actual segment. Demographics would have missed everything that mattered. The JTBD generator fires the flag, forces the reframe, and makes the hypothesis testable before the audience is built on invented data.
beats.visual_intent: Summary: milkshake job framing at top; three job-level segment names below; bottom: 'demographics describe, JTBD predicts — segment by job'

beats.role_note: HANDOFF LAW — prompt READ aloud AND discussed; greeting 'Your turn.'
beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste your brand archetype and any qualitative data you have — interview notes, support tickets, forum threads — and run the generator. When it fires the demographic flag, do not override it. Work the reframe until the segment name is a phrase describing what people hire your brand to do. Then read the evidence status tags. Any claim marked needs-evidence is a research task, not a fact. That is the list you take into next week's user interviews.

props.command:
claude "generate 3 JTBD segments for my brand:
  Archetype: [YOUR ARCHETYPE]
  Qualitative data: [INTERVIEW QUOTES / FORUM THREADS /
    SUPPORT TICKET PATTERNS]
  Fire [DEMOGRAPHIC — CONVERT TO JOB] on any segment
  leading with age, income, or occupation.
  Tag every claim CONFIRMED / NEEDS-EVIDENCE / INFERRED."

beats.narration_text: Build a JTBD Audience Segment Generator with Claude. This is Liam, in for Bear — build it with Claude, then take it apart.
